title-group.article-title: Social long COVID: impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic on public health and policy in Brazil

contrib.aff: Center of Science and Society, Columbia University, New York, NY10027, USA

author-notes.corresp: Corresponding author. Center of Science and Society. 511 Fayerweather Hall, 1180 Amsterdam Avenue, New York, NY 10027, United States. E-mail: np2804@columbia.edu

article-meta.issue-title: Impact of Covid in Brazil: the EPICOVID 2.0 Project

permissions.copyright-statement: © The Author(s) 2025. Published by Oxford University Press on behalf of the International Epidemiological Association.

For commercial re-use, please contact reprints@oup.com for reprints and translation rights for reprints. All other permissions can be obtained through our RightsLink service via the Permissions link on the article page on our site—for further information please contact journals.permissions@oup.com.

body.p: Pandemic impacts and preparedness have been at the center of public debate for the past few years. Governments recognize the need for more metrics and investment in preparedness, and are generally in agreement when it comes to discussing the impacts of school closings, vaccine mandates, and the need for more investment in localized vaccine production and drug development. The social aspects of the pandemic, how it impacted different sectors of society, and the role of social inequalities, wealth distribution, demographics, and education have not received the same attention. Some authors have indeed searched the literature about past pandemics and found that most studies focused on health impacts and few looked at the interaction between health and social aspects. Also, they point out that few studies compared the social aspects of different pandemics, or even the same pandemic in different sites or contexts [1].

Likewise, research on the impacts of historical pandemics show that the social changes brought about by the burden of the disease itself, including changes in the demographics of the population, depending on the characteristics of the most vulnerable, the public perception and cooperation, or lack thereof, and the implementation of preventive and combative measures by the government, can be significant and alter the landscapes of societies. Examples include how the Black Death resulted in changes to the feudal society and labor laws, how the smallpox outbreaks in the Americas paved the way for European colonization, and how the Spanish flu—probably together with World War I—was particularly deadly for young men, causing a huge labor shortage and economic crisis, but also created the opportunity to advance women’s rights [2, 3].

COVID-19 research has advanced remarkably in vaccine development, testing, and even in identifying the technical needs for pandemic preparedness in the future, such as investments in local production and distribution, and better training for healthcare workers. This means that our institutions might be better prepared. But what are the social changes brought about by the COVID-19 pandemic? And what can we learn from these changes that can help us prepare society for the next health emergency? What metrics can we develop to improve our risk assessment of vaccine and mask mandates, social-distancing protocols, and school closings? If we do not have a good understanding of how society reacts to prevention and mitigation measures, and the impact of both the disease and the interventions on people’s capacity to work, study, and support their families, then how can we make accurate risk assessments and come up with interventions that will balance health and social impacts?

A group of Brazilian scientists stood up to the challenge and asked these questions. The result is a suite of four papers investigating the impacts of COVID-19. The first dealt with the perception and acceptance of vaccines, including but not restricted to COVID-19 [4]; the second investigated self-reported case prevalence and hospitalization in different localities and demographics [5]; the third was a countrywide study on the social impact of COVID-19 on Brazilian families [6]; and finally, the last one measured self-reported long COVID symptoms and tried to identify the most vulnerable settings and populations [7].
The group had already published a seminal paper in 2020 that mapped COVID-19 antibody prevalence as an indirect measure of positivity [8].

The new papers add relevant information to the previous research. Assessing vaccine uptake and acceptance for both COVID-19 and influenza vaccines, Silveira and collaborators highlight that the Northern region still shows great vulnerability, presenting with the lowest coverages for both vaccines. Coverage for the COVID-19 vaccine was higher for White people and women, and correlated with levels of education and wealth. It is tempting to jump to a conclusion that educated people have higher acceptance of the vaccine, but access could be playing a major role here, too. The authors investigated the role of religious affiliations and found that vaccine uptake is lower among evangelical communities, who have a strong presence in the North.

Perhaps the most relevant of the four papers is the one by Pellanda et al. [6], which investigated the social impacts of the pandemic, including the death of a family member, job losses, school attendance, and food security. Not surprisingly, households led by women were the most affected. The results show us that there is more to pandemic preparedness than vaccines, medications, and healthcare structures. Social preparedness is also essential to tackle the impacts of the preventive measures, restrictions, and mandates, and to make sure that people are not penalized for collaborating. Policy authors have highlighted the importance of developing social interventions and metrics in health policies [9]. One lesson we should learn from COVID-19 is that the long-term impacts are not restricted to direct health effects. The indirect cost of both the disease and the disease management must be a part of the preparedness equation.

Social aspects of pandemic preparedness should be a key component of the public policies toolkit. While technical developments are essential and governments need to focus on policies to guarantee affordable vaccines, medications, and healthcare, the social structure should not be overlooked. We need policies to make sure that we are well equipped to deal not only with the burden of the disease itself, but also with the collateral damage it causes to society in terms of work capacity, food security, and education. Social sciences must be regularly incorporated into epidemiology science.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: The author thanks Professors Pedro Halal and Cesar Victora for the invitation to write this commentary.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The author did not receive any funding for this manuscript.

sec.title: Use of artificial intelligence (AI) tools
sec.p: No AI tools were used in this manuscript.
